title: The German insurance market: opportunities and considerations for fund managers
author: Silverlake Financial Services Group
summary: Germany's insurance market is a massive capital pool of approximately €1.9 trillion. We analyse the regulations (Solvency II and AnlV) and alternative asset class allocations that fund managers must understand to successfully raise capital.
content:
Germany’s insurance market is one of the largest and most sophisticated institutional capital pools in Europe, managing approximately €1.9 trillion in assets. For international fund managers seeking institutional capital, understanding this landscape is a key prerequisite to successful fundraising.

The Growth of Alternative Allocations

In recent years, German insurance companies and pension funds (known as Pensionskassen or Versorgungwerke) have steadily increased their allocations to alternative investments. Over the last two years alone, institutional alternative allocations have grown from roughly 23% to over 30% of total portfolios. This trend is driven by a prolonged hunt for yield and a structural shift towards private markets, including private equity, private credit, infrastructure, and commercial real estate debt.

Navigating the Regulatory Landscape

A crucial factor for fund managers to grasp is that German insurers are not governed by a single regulatory system. Instead, they are split into two distinct regimes:

  1. Solvency II: This European-wide framework applies to larger, primary insurance groups. Under Solvency II, capital charges are determined based on the risk profile of the assets. Consequently, structured alternative assets require careful design to avoid prohibitive capital charges.
  2. The Investment Regulation (Anlageverordnung - AnlV): This domestic German regime applies to smaller insurance companies, specific pension funds, and professional registries. The AnlV imposes strict quantitative limits on specific asset classes and requires funds to meet detailed eligibility criteria to qualify as eligible assets.

The Significance of the AnlV

Although it technically applies only to smaller entities, the influence of the AnlV is far-reaching. It is estimated that more than one-third of the institutional capital in Germany aligns with the AnlV. This is because large insurance groups often voluntarily manage portions of their portfolios (such as their pension assets) in accordance with AnlV rules to ensure consistency and domestic regulatory approval.

Key Takeaways for Fund Managers

Managers targeting German insurance capital should focus on two main strategies:

  • Customized Structuring: Establishing parallel fund vehicles or feeder funds, such as German specialized institutional funds (Spezial-AIF) or securitised notes, is often necessary to comply with the technical requirements of Solvency II and AnlV.
  • Familiarity with Reporting: Insurers require highly detailed regulatory reporting (including Solvency II Solvency Capital Requirement calculations and VAG reporting templates). Providing these templates seamlessly can be a major differentiator during the due diligence process.

title: Countdown to the loan origination regime in AIFMD II
author: Silverlake Credit Funds Group
summary: With the loan origination regime under AIFMD II due to take effect on 16 April 2026, alternative fund managers must prepare for new leverage limits, risk retention requirements, and diversification rules.
content:
The legislative countdown is reaching its final weeks. The Alternative Investment Fund Managers Directive II (AIFMD II) framework introduces the European Union's first harmonised regime for loan-originating alternative investment funds (AIFs). The rules are due to take effect on 16 April 2026, subject to member state implementation, marking a major turning point for the European private credit sector.

Why the New Rules Matter

Until now, private debt funds operating in Europe faced a fragmented patchwork of national rules. Some jurisdictions, like Ireland and Luxembourg, established specific loan-origination frameworks, while others prohibited direct lending by non-bank entities altogether. AIFMD II creates a unified European passport for loan-originating AIFs, but it accompanies this freedom with strict harmonised risk-management constraints.

Core Pillars of the Loan Origination Regime

Alternative fund managers must align their operations with several key regulatory pillars before the April deadline:

  1. Leverage Limits: Open-ended loan-originating AIFs will face a leverage limit of 175% (calculated using the commitment method), while closed-ended funds will be capped at 300%. These limits are intended to prevent systemic risk while allowing debt funds to use structural leverage.
  2. Risk Retention: To align incentives, AIFs must retain 5% of the nominal value of each loan they originate and subsequently sell on the secondary market. This 'skin-in-the-game' rule is designed to prevent the 'originate-to-distribute' model that contributed to the 2008 financial crisis.
  3. Diversification Limits: An AIF cannot lend more than 20% of its capital to a single borrower if the borrower is a financial institution, an AIF, or a UCITS.
  4. Conflicts of Interest: Direct prohibitions will be introduced preventing AIFs from lending to their AIFM, their staff, their depositary, or entities within the same corporate group.

Steps for Fund Managers to Take Now

As the implementation date of 16 April 2026 approaches, credit managers must review their existing fund documents and structures. While certain grandfathering provisions apply to existing loans and funds, new fund launches and future credit originations will need to build these restrictions directly into their constitutional documents and risk monitoring systems.

title: Real opportunities: how private capital can access real estate
author: Silverlake Real Estate Group
summary: Recent corrections in the European commercial real estate (CRE) market have unlocked significant entry points for private capital. We explore alternative routes, from direct equity partnerships to the booming real estate credit sector.
content:
The European commercial real estate (CRE) market has undergone a significant valuation correction over the last two years. This repricing, combined with traditional banks retrenching from property lending, has created a compelling landscape for private capital. As we move deeper into 2026, private capital investors are uniquely positioned to capture value across the real estate spectrum.

The Shifting Capital Stack

Historically, commercial banks provided up to 70% or 80% Loan-to-Value (LTV) financing for high-quality properties. Today, bank lending parameters have tightened significantly, with LTVs often capped at 50% or 60%. This funding gap has created two parallel opportunities for private capital:

  • Real Estate Credit: Private debt funds are filling the vacuum, providing senior loans, mezzanine debt, and preferred equity to sponsors who need to refinance existing properties or fund new acquisitions. This provides investors with stable, downside-protected yields.
  • Equity Co-investments: Revalued assets are creating attractive entry points for opportunistic equity investors. Private sponsors are partnering with family offices and sovereign wealth funds to acquire high-quality, ESG-compliant office, logistics, and residential spaces at discounts to replacement cost.

Balancing Stakeholder Interests

Success in this repriced market requires a sophisticated approach to structuring. With multiple layers of debt and equity in the capital stack, negotiation of intercreditor agreements is critical. Private lenders must secure robust covenant packages, control rights, and clear paths to enforcement, while equity sponsors require the operational flexibility to reposition assets (such as converting offices into residential spaces or upgrading energy efficiency ratings to meet strict environmental standards).

Alternative Access Routes

For private capital investors, real estate is no longer just about owning brick-and-mortar buildings. Alternative access routes, including joint ventures, developer partnerships, and secondary purchases of real estate fund interests, offer flexibility and liquidity. Furthermore, the rise of specialized sub-sectors—such as data centres, student housing, and life science labs—allows capital to target secular thematic growth rather than generic office or retail footprints.

A Look Ahead

As central banks stabilise interest rates, transaction volumes in the real estate sector are beginning to recover. For forward-thinking investors, the current vintage of real estate debt and equity allocations represents a rare alignment of attractive entry valuations and strong legal protection, establishing real estate as a key focus area for private capital allocation in 2026.
